teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Under s.14(2) of the Sale of Goods Act 1979, where goods are sold in the course of a business, there is an implied term that the goods supplied under the contract are of satisfactory quality.

**The Objective Standard:** 'Satisfactory quality' is measured against the standard that a reasonable person would regard as satisfactory, taking account of any description of the goods, the price (if relevant), and all other relevant circumstances — s.14(2A). This is not the buyer's subjective opinion; it is an objective benchmark.

**Aspects of Quality — s.14(2B):** The following factors are among those to be considered: (a) fitness for all the purposes for which goods of that kind are commonly supplied; (b) appearance and finish; (c) freedom from minor defects; (d) safety; and (e) durability. A single significant defect is sufficient to render goods unsatisfactory, even if other aspects are acceptable.

**Exclusions — s.14(2C):** The implied term does not extend to: (i) matters specifically drawn to the buyer's attention before the contract is made; or (ii) where the buyer examines the goods before the contract, defects which that examination ought to have revealed.

**In short:** If a business sells goods, the law implies they meet a reasonable standard of quality. The price paid sets an expectation floor — a discount item may tolerate minor imperfections, but it must still be safe, fit for common purposes, and durable for a reasonable period.

question: Under s.14(2) of the Sale of Goods Act 1979, where goods are sold in the course of a business, there is an implied term that the goods supplied under the contract are of __________ quality.
feedback: Correct. SGA 1979, s.14(2) — the standard is 'satisfactory quality,' replacing the older 'merchantable quality' under the Supply of Goods (Implied Terms) Act 1973. The standard is objective: what a reasonable person would regard as satisfactory — s.14(2A).

question: In Rogers v __________ (Scarborough) Ltd [1987] QB 933, the Court of Appeal held that a new Range Rover with oil leaks, defective paintwork, and engine problems was not of satisfactory quality under s.14(2) SGA 1979, despite having been repaired by the dealer.
feedback: Correct. *Rogers v Parish (Scarborough) Ltd* [1987] QB 933 — the defects cumulatively meant the vehicle was not of satisfactory quality. The fact that repairs were possible did not cure the breach; the obligation is that goods be of satisfactory quality at the time of delivery.

question: Match each aspect of 'satisfactory quality' under s.14(2B) SGA 1979 to its description:
answer: Fitness for common purposes=Goods must be fit for all purposes for which goods of that kind are commonly supplied; Appearance and finish=The visual and tactile quality, including freedom from scratches, dents, or poor finishing; Freedom from minor defects=Goods must not suffer from small but material flaws that affect usability or value; Safety=Goods must not pose risks to persons or property when used as intended; Durability=Goods must last for a reasonable period given their nature and price

options.text: Goods must be fit for all purposes for which goods of that kind are commonly supplied
The visual and tactile quality, including freedom from scratches, dents, or poor finishing
Goods must not suffer from small but material flaws that affect usability or value
Goods must not pose risks to persons or property when used as intended
Goods must last for a reasonable period given their nature and price

feedback: Well matched. All five aspects in s.14(2B) inform the test, but a single significant defect in any one aspect can render goods unsatisfactory. The weight given to each factor depends on the nature of the goods and the price.

question: OfficePro Ltd buys 50 office chairs from a business supplier for £30 each — a budget range. Within two months, the hydraulic lifts on 12 chairs fail and the fabric on most chairs has pilled badly. The supplier argues, 'At £30 a chair, you cannot expect premium quality.' Applying s.14(2) SGA 1979:

options.text: OfficePro has no claim — the low price means no standard of quality is implied
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.14(2) applies to all business sales regardless of price. Price is a factor in assessing what is 'satisfactory,' but it does not eliminate the implied term.

options.text: OfficePro has no claim — business-to-business sales are caveat emptor
options.feedback: Incorrect. The implied term under s.14(2) applies to all sales 'in the course of a business,' whether the buyer is a consumer or another business.

options.text: The chairs are likely not of satisfactory quality — even budget chairs should remain functional beyond two months; price informs but does not eliminate the durability and fitness requirements under s.14(2B)
options.feedback: Correct. Under s.14(2A), price is relevant to what a reasonable person would regard as satisfactory, but a budget chair that fails within two months is unlikely to meet even a lowered objective standard. Durability (s.14(2B)(e)) and fitness for common purposes (s.14(2B)(a)) are breached.

options.text: The supplier is liable only if OfficePro inspected the chairs before purchase
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.14(2C)(b) may exclude liability for defects that a pre-contract inspection ought to have revealed, but inspection is not a precondition to the implied term arising. Here, the defects were latent and would not have been apparent on inspection.

feedback: Price is a relevant factor under s.14(2A), but it does not excuse goods that fail in their basic function or durability. A reasonable person would regard chairs that break within two months as unsatisfactory, even at a budget price. The s.14(2B) factors — particularly durability and fitness for common purposes — set a floor below which no goods should fall.

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'The implied term of satisfactory quality under s.14(2) SGA 1979 requires that goods be of the highest possible quality, judged by the buyer's own personal standards. If the buyer subjectively believes the goods are sub-standard, the seller is automatically in breach. The price paid is irrelevant to the assessment.'
answer: Satisfactory quality is an objective standard — what a reasonable person would regard as satisfactory, not the buyer's subjective view. Price is expressly relevant under s.14(2A). The standard is not 'highest possible' but 'satisfactory.'
feedback: Found it. s.14(2A) states that quality is measured against 'the standard that a reasonable person would regard as satisfactory, taking account of ... the price (if relevant).' The test is objective, not subjective. The buyer's personal opinion is not decisive — a reasonable person's assessment, informed by all circumstances including price and description, controls.

question: Under s.14(2B) of the Sale of Goods Act 1979, which THREE of the following are expressly listed as aspects of the quality of goods?

options.text: Appearance and finish
options.feedback: Correct. s.14(2B)(b) — this includes freedom from scratches, dents, and poor paintwork.

options.text: Whether the goods were the cheapest available on the market
options.feedback: Incorrect. Price is a factor in assessing what a reasonable person would regard as satisfactory (s.14(2A)), but it is not itself an aspect of quality listed in s.14(2B).

options.text: Safety
options.feedback: Correct. s.14(2B)(d) — goods must not pose risks to persons or property. Safety is a fundamental aspect of quality.

options.text: Whether the goods were manufactured in the UK
options.feedback: Incorrect. The origin of manufacture is not listed in s.14(2B). It is not a statutory aspect of quality under SGA 1979.

options.text: Durability
options.feedback: Correct. s.14(2B)(e) — goods must last for a reasonable period. What is 'reasonable' depends on the nature of the goods and their price.

feedback: The correct three are (a), (c), and (e). The full list in s.14(2B) is: fitness for common purposes, appearance and finish, freedom from minor defects, safety, and durability. Price is a factor in the overall assessment under s.14(2A), but not a listed aspect of quality itself.

question: A second-hand electronics dealer sells a refurbished laptop to Sam for £150, describing it as 'fully working.' Sam inspects the laptop in the shop and notices a small scratch on the lid but buys it anyway. At home, Sam discovers the battery lasts only 15 minutes — it should last 4 hours. The dealer says, 'You inspected it — you accepted it as-is.' Applying s.14(2) and (2C) SGA 1979:

options.text: The dealer is not liable — Sam examined the goods, so s.14(2C)(b) excludes liability for any defect
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.14(2C)(b) only excludes defects that the examination 'ought to have revealed.' A battery defect would not be apparent from a visual inspection in the shop.

options.text: The dealer is liable — the battery defect was latent and would not have been revealed by a reasonable shop inspection; the scratch was apparent but does not excuse the undiscoverable battery fault
options.feedback: Correct. s.14(2C)(b) excludes only defects that the buyer's examination ought to have revealed. A battery's capacity cannot be assessed by a visual inspection. The scratch (an apparent defect) may be excluded; the battery fault is not.

options.text: The dealer is not liable because second-hand goods are never subject to s.14(2)
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.14(2) applies to ALL goods sold in the course of a business, including second-hand goods. The price and description may lower the expected standard, but the implied term still applies.

options.text: The dealer is liable for the scratch but not the battery, because the battery is a separate component
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.14(2) applies to 'the goods supplied under the contract' as a whole. A latent defect in a component (the battery) renders the goods collectively unsatisfactory if it materially affects use.

feedback: s.14(2C)(b) is a narrow exclusion: it only applies to defects that the examination *ought* to have revealed. A reasonable pre-purchase inspection of a laptop in a shop would check for physical damage (like the scratch), but not battery life. The battery defect was latent and is covered by the s.14(2) implied term. The price and the 'second-hand' description lower the standard of what is 'satisfactory,' but a 15-minute battery life on a laptop sold as 'fully working' falls below any reasonable standard.

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Under s.14(3) of the Sale of Goods Act 1979, where the buyer expressly or by implication makes known to the seller any particular purpose for which the goods are being bought, there is an implied term that the goods supplied are reasonably fit for that purpose — whether or not that is a purpose for which such goods are commonly supplied.

**Particular Purpose & Reliance:** The buyer must make known the particular purpose, and must rely (or it must be reasonable to rely) on the seller's skill or judgment. The reliance need not be total or exclusive — partial reliance is sufficient. The exception is where the buyer does not rely, or it is unreasonable for the buyer to rely, on the seller's skill or judgment — s.14(3).

**Key Case — Baldry v Marshall [1925] 1 KB 260:** The buyer told the seller he wanted a car 'suitable for touring.' The seller recommended a Bugatti. The Bugatti was a racing car, wholly unsuitable for touring. The Court of Appeal held there was a breach of the (equivalent predecessor to) s.14(3) — the buyer made known a particular purpose and relied on the seller's expertise.

**Key Case — Grant v Australian Knitting Mills [1936] AC 85:** The buyer purchased woolen underwear from a retailer. The underwear contained excess sulphites from the manufacturing process, causing severe dermatitis. The Privy Council held there was a breach of BOTH s.14(2) (satisfactory quality — underwear that causes injury is not satisfactory) and s.14(3) (fitness for the particular purpose of being worn). The purpose need not be 'particular' in the sense of unusual — the ordinary purpose of being worn on skin is sufficient.

**In short:** s.14(3) protects the buyer who communicates a specific need and relies on the seller to meet it. The seller who recommends goods implicitly warrants that they are fit for that communicated purpose. The purpose can be ordinary (wearing underwear) or specialist (a touring car); what matters is that the buyer made it known and relied on the seller.
oscoaReferences: Grant v Australian Knitting Mills [1936] AC 85

question: Under s.____(3) of the Sale of Goods Act 1979, where the buyer makes known a particular purpose to the seller and relies on the seller's skill or judgment, there is an implied term that the goods are reasonably fit for that purpose.
feedback: Correct. SGA 1979, s.14(3) — the implied term of fitness for a particular purpose. The provision has two key requirements: (a) the particular purpose is made known to the seller, and (b) the buyer relies on the seller's skill or judgment. Partial reliance suffices — the buyer need not rely exclusively.

question: In __________ v Marshall [1925] 1 KB 260, the Court of Appeal held that a seller who recommended a Bugatti to a buyer who had specified he wanted a car 'suitable for touring' breached the implied term of fitness for purpose.
feedback: Correct. The full citation is *Baldry v Marshall* [1925] 1 KB 260. The buyer expressly made known his particular purpose (touring) and relied on the seller's recommendation. The Bugatti was unsuitable — a racing car, not a touring car. This remains a leading authority on s.14(3).

question: Place the key events of *Baldry v Marshall* [1925] in the correct sequence as established by the Court of Appeal:
answer: Buyer tells seller he needs a car suitable for touring → Seller recommends a Bugatti → Buyer purchases the Bugatti in reliance on seller's judgment → Bugatti proves unsuitable — it is a racing car → Court holds seller breached implied term of fitness for purpose (now s.14(3) SGA 1979)

options.text: Buyer expressly tells seller he needs a car 'suitable for touring'
Seller recommends a Bugatti, relying on his superior knowledge of motor vehicles
Buyer purchases the Bugatti in reliance on seller's skill and judgment
Bugatti proves wholly unsuitable — it is a racing car, not a touring car
Court holds seller breached implied term of fitness for purpose (now s.14(3) SGA 1979)

feedback: Correct. *Baldry v Marshall* [1925] 1 KB 260 established that where a buyer makes known a particular purpose and relies on the seller's recommendation, the seller impliedly warrants that the goods are reasonably fit for that purpose. The seller's superior knowledge is a key factor in establishing reliance.

question: FarmFresh Ltd tells a tractor dealer: 'We need a tractor that can handle steep, muddy hills on our farm in Wales.' The dealer recommends a specific model. FarmFresh buys it. The tractor's engine stalls repeatedly on inclines, making it unsafe. The dealer says, 'It's a general-purpose tractor — you didn't say you needed a specialist model.' Applying s.14(3) SGA 1979 and *Baldry v Marshall* [1925]:

options.text: The dealer is in breach — FarmFresh communicated a particular purpose (steep, muddy hills) and relied on the dealer's recommendation; the dealer's expertise imposes an obligation to supply goods reasonably fit for that stated purpose
options.feedback: Correct. Under s.14(3) and *Baldry v Marshall* [1925], once a particular purpose is made known and the seller recommends goods, the seller warrants fitness for that purpose. The dealer should have recommended a suitable model or declined to do so.

options.text: No breach — FarmFresh should have employed an independent engineer to assess the tractor's suitability
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.14(3) specifically protects the buyer who relies on the *seller's* skill or judgment. There is no requirement to obtain independent advice.

options.text: No breach — FarmFresh did not specify the exact gradient of the hills
options.feedback: Incorrect. The buyer need only make known the *nature* of the purpose, not every technical detail. 'Steep, muddy hills' is sufficient to trigger the implied term.

options.text: The dealer is in breach only if the tractor was unfit for all purposes, not just FarmFresh's particular one
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.14(3) is specifically concerned with the *particular* purpose made known, not purposes generally. A tractor fit for flat fields may still breach s.14(3) if it cannot handle the hills the buyer specified.

feedback: The core principle from *Baldry v Marshall* [1925] is that once a buyer communicates a particular purpose and the seller makes a recommendation, the seller assumes responsibility for the goods' fitness for that purpose. The seller cannot escape liability by claiming the goods were fit for some other, unstated purpose. FarmFresh is entitled to damages for breach of the s.14(3) implied term.

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'Section 14(3) SGA 1979 only applies where the buyer communicates an unusual or specialist purpose. If the buyer is purchasing goods for their ordinary, everyday use, s.14(3) does not apply — the buyer must instead rely on s.14(2). Furthermore, the buyer must have relied exclusively on the seller's skill and judgment, and any independent research by the buyer defeats the claim.'
answer: s.14(3) applies to ANY particular purpose, including ordinary purposes. The purpose need not be unusual — *Grant v Australian Knitting Mills* [1936] confirms that the ordinary purpose of wearing underwear is a 'particular purpose.' Reliance need not be exclusive — partial reliance suffices.
feedback: Found two errors. First, s.14(3) applies to any particular purpose — the purpose need not be unusual or specialist. In *Grant v Australian Knitting Mills* [1936] AC 85, the Privy Council held that the purpose of wearing underwear on skin was a 'particular purpose' for s.14(3), even though it was the most ordinary purpose imaginable. Second, reliance need not be exclusive — partial reliance is sufficient. The buyer may undertake their own research and still rely on the seller's recommendation. The question is whether the seller's skill or judgment formed part of the basis of the buyer's decision.
oscoaReferences: Grant v Australian Knitting Mills [1936] AC 85

question: Under s.14(3) SGA 1979 and the case law, which TWO of the following are required to establish the implied term of fitness for a particular purpose?

options.text: The buyer must make known to the seller the particular purpose for which the goods are required
options.feedback: Correct. s.14(3) — the buyer must, expressly or by implication, make the particular purpose known. This is the trigger for the implied term.

options.text: The buyer must have relied exclusively on the seller's skill or judgment, with no independent research
options.feedback: Incorrect. Partial reliance is sufficient. *Cammell Laird & Co Ltd v Manganese Bronze and Brass Co Ltd* [1934] AC 402 — the buyer may rely partly on the seller and partly on their own judgment.

options.text: The buyer must rely, or it must be reasonable for the buyer to rely, on the seller's skill or judgment
options.feedback: Correct. s.14(3) — the buyer must show reliance, but partial reliance is enough. The reliance must be reasonable; if the seller disclaims expertise and tells the buyer to get independent advice, reliance may be unreasonable.

options.text: The purpose must be unusual or specialist, not an ordinary purpose
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Grant v Australian Knitting Mills* [1936] AC 85 — the ordinary purpose of wearing underwear on skin satisfies s.14(3). The purpose need only be 'particular' in the sense of being the specific purpose the buyer communicates.

options.text: The buyer must prove that the goods are also unsatisfactory under s.14(2) before s.14(3) can apply
options.feedback: Incorrect. ss.14(2) and 14(3) are independent implied terms. A buyer can succeed under s.14(3) alone if the goods are unfit for the particular purpose. *Grant* [1936] found independent breaches of both, but either suffices.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (c). The two core requirements of s.14(3) are communication of the particular purpose and reliance on the seller's skill or judgment. Reliance need not be exclusive, and the purpose need not be unusual — the everyday purpose of wearing clothing (*Grant* [1936]) satisfies the test.
oscoaReferences: Grant v Australian Knitting Mills [1936] AC 85
Cammell Laird & Co Ltd v Manganese Bronze and Brass Co Ltd [1934] AC 402

question: Priya emails a software vendor: 'I need an accounting package that handles multi-currency transactions for at least 50 currencies.' The vendor replies, 'Our Premium Package does exactly that — it's perfect for you.' Priya buys it. She later discovers it handles only 5 currencies. The vendor argues, 'Our website's small print says you should verify specifications before purchase.' Applying s.14(3) SGA 1979:

options.text: Priya has no claim — the website disclaimer defeats reliance under s.14(3)
options.feedback: Incorrect. A generic website disclaimer does not automatically defeat reliance when the seller has made a specific, express representation in direct response to the buyer's stated purpose.

options.text: The vendor is in breach — Priya made known a particular purpose, the vendor specifically recommended the Premium Package for that purpose, and the website small print does not displace the specific reliance on the vendor's express assurance
options.feedback: Correct. The vendor's express response — 'does exactly that' — is a specific recommendation that s.14(3) covers. A general website disclaimer is unlikely to override a specific, direct representation. Priya relied on the vendor's skill and judgment, and it was reasonable to do so.

options.text: Priya has no claim because software is not 'goods' under the SGA 1979
options.feedback: Incorrect. While the SGA 1979 applies to 'goods' (tangible property), the Software Regulations and SGA 1979 can apply to software supplied on a tangible medium. Even if not, equivalent terms in the Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982 or CRA 2015 would apply. The legal principle is the same: implied fitness for stated purpose.

options.text: Priya has a claim only if she can also prove the software is of unsatisfactory quality under s.14(2)
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.14(3) stands independently. Even if the software were perfectly functional (and thus of 'satisfactory quality' under s.14(2)), the vendor's failure to meet the stated purpose is a distinct breach of s.14(3).

feedback: The specific, direct representation by the seller ('does exactly that') is the key. Under s.14(3) and *Baldry v Marshall* [1925], when a buyer communicates a particular purpose and the seller recommends goods by name, the seller warrants fitness for that purpose. A generic website disclaimer buried in small print is unlikely to displace the reliance created by an express, tailored recommendation. This reflects the principle that clearer, more specific terms prevail over general boilerplate.

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** The implied terms in ss.12–15 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979 are powerful protections, but sellers often seek to exclude or modify them by contract. The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (UCTA) and the Consumer Rights Act 2015 (CRA) regulate the extent to which these terms can be excluded.

**UCTA 1977 — s.6:** Section 6 applies to liability arising under ss.12–15 SGA 1979:

- **s.6(1):** Liability for breach of s.12 (the seller's right to sell, quiet possession, freedom from encumbrances) cannot be excluded or restricted by any contract term whatsoever. Title is fundamental — you cannot sell what you do not own and disclaim responsibility.
- **s.6(1A) (business→consumer):** Liability for breach of ss.13–15 cannot be excluded or restricted as against a person dealing as consumer. (This is now largely governed by CRA 2015, ss.31 and 57, which render such exclusion terms not binding on consumers.)
- **s.6(2) (business→business):** As against a person dealing *otherwise than* as consumer, liability for breach of ss.13–15 may be excluded or restricted, but only insofar as the term satisfies the requirement of reasonableness.

**The Reasonableness Test — UCTA 1977, s.11 and Schedule 2:** A term is reasonable only if it was 'a fair and reasonable one to be included having regard to the circumstances which were, or ought reasonably to have been, known to or in the contemplation of the parties when the contract was made.' Schedule 2 guidelines include: the relative bargaining strength of the parties; whether the buyer received an inducement to agree; whether the buyer knew or ought to have known of the term; whether the goods were manufactured to the buyer's special order; and whether compliance with the term was practicable.

**Business vs Consumer — A Critical Distinction:**
- **Business-to-consumer:** CRA 2015, s.31 makes any term excluding ss.9–17 CRA (the consumer equivalents of ss.13–15 SGA) not binding. A business cannot exclude liability for satisfactory quality or fitness for purpose against a consumer, full stop.
- **Business-to-business:** UCTA 1977, s.6(2) permits exclusion of ss.13–15, but only if the reasonableness test is met. This is a higher bar than many businesses realise — boilerplate exclusion clauses in standard terms are frequently struck down as unreasonable.

**In short:** s.12 (title) can never be excluded. ss.13–15 (description, quality, fitness) cannot be excluded against consumers at all (CRA 2015). Against other businesses, exclusion is possible but only if the court finds the term reasonable — a fact-sensitive inquiry that often defeats broadly-drafted exclusion clauses.
oscoaReferences: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, ss.6, 11, Sch 2

question: Under s.____(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, liability for breach of the obligations arising under s.12 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979 (the seller's right to sell) cannot be excluded or restricted by any contract term.
feedback: Correct. UCTA 1977, s.6(1) — s.12 SGA 1979 (title) is sacrosanct. A seller cannot sell goods it does not own and contract out of liability for doing so. This applies regardless of whether the buyer is a consumer or a business.

question: In George __________ (Chesterhall) Ltd v Finney Lock Seeds Ltd [1983] 2 AC 803, the House of Lords held that an exclusion clause limiting the seller's liability to the price of defective cabbage seed was unreasonable under UCTA 1977 and unenforceable.
feedback: Correct. *George Mitchell (Chesterhall) Ltd v Finney Lock Seeds Ltd* [1983] 2 AC 803 — the seed was defective and the exclusion clause (limiting liability to the seed's purchase price) was held unreasonable. The buyers lost an entire crop worth far more. The case is a leading authority on the application of the UCTA reasonableness test.
oscoaReferences: George Mitchell (Chesterhall) Ltd v Finney Lock Seeds Ltd [1983] 2 AC 803

question: Match each exclusion scenario to the correct legal rule under UCTA 1977 and CRA 2015:
answer: Business seller excludes s.12 SGA 1979 (title)=Cannot be excluded — UCTA 1977 s.6(1) — void absolutely; Business seller excludes ss.13–15 against a consumer=Cannot be excluded — CRA 2015 s.31 — not binding on consumer; Business seller excludes ss.13–15 against another business=May be excluded only if the term satisfies the reasonableness test — UCTA 1977 s.6(2); Business seller excludes s.14 against a consumer=Not binding on the consumer — CRA 2015 ss.31, 57 — no need to assess reasonableness

options.text: Cannot be excluded — UCTA 1977 s.6(1) — void absolutely
Cannot be excluded — CRA 2015 s.31 — not binding on consumer
options.pair: Business seller excludes ss.13–15 against a consumer

options.text: May be excluded only if the term satisfies the reasonableness test — UCTA 1977 s.6(2)
options.pair: Business seller excludes ss.13–15 against another business

options.text: Not binding on the consumer — CRA 2015 ss.31, 57 — no need to assess reasonableness
options.pair: Business seller excludes s.14 against a consumer

feedback: Well matched. The key hierarchy: (1) s.12 SGA (title) — never excludable, against anyone. (2) Consumer sales — ss.13–15 cannot be excluded at all under CRA 2015. (3) Business-to-business — ss.13–15 exclusion is permissible but must be reasonable under UCTA 1977 s.6(2).

question: BrewCo Ltd (a large brewery) supplies beer to Finlay's Pub (a small independent pub). The supply contract includes a clause: 'All implied terms under ss.13–15 SGA 1979 are excluded. Liability for any defect is limited to the price of the individual barrel.' A barrel supplied is contaminated and makes several customers ill. Finlay's loses £15,000 in lost trade and faces compensation claims. The barrel price was £120. Applying UCTA 1977, s.6(2):

options.text: The exclusion clause is valid — businesses are free to contract on whatever terms they choose
options.feedback: Incorrect. Business-to-business contracts are subject to UCTA 1977. s.6(2) requires any exclusion of ss.13–15 to satisfy the reasonableness test.

options.text: The exclusion clause is automatically void because ss.13–15 can never be excluded
options.feedback: Incorrect. Unlike s.12, ss.13–15 can be excluded in business-to-business contracts — but only if the term is reasonable under s.6(2). The court must evaluate.

options.text: The clause is likely unreasonable under UCTA 1977 s.6(2) — the significant inequality in bargaining power, the fact the clause was in standard terms, and the disproportionate cap (£120 vs £15,000+ loss) render it unreasonable
options.feedback: Correct. Under Schedule 2 to UCTA 1977, the court will weigh the relative bargaining strength and whether the buyer knew of the term. A large brewery imposing standard terms on a small pub, with a derisory liability cap, is unlikely to satisfy the reasonableness test. *George Mitchell* [1983] supports this analysis.

options.text: The clause is reasonable because the barrel price was only £120
options.feedback: Incorrect. The low unit price is one factor, but it must be weighed against the gravity of the breach (contaminated food causing illness), the inequality of bargaining power, and the foreseeability of losses. The Schedule 2 factors collectively point away from reasonableness here.

feedback: The reasonableness test under UCTA 1977 s.11 and Schedule 2 is a multi-factorial assessment. Key factors here: (1) inequality of bargaining power (large brewery vs small pub), (2) the clause was in standard terms not individually negotiated, (3) the buyer may not have known or understood the exclusion, (4) the liability cap (£120) is grossly disproportionate to foreseeable losses (£15,000+). Under *George Mitchell (Chesterhall) Ltd v Finney Lock Seeds Ltd* [1983] 2 AC 803, such a clause is highly unlikely to be reasonable.
oscoaReferences: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, ss.6(2), 11, Sch 2
George Mitchell (Chesterhall) Ltd v Finney Lock Seeds Ltd [1983] 2 AC 803

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'A seller can always exclude liability under ss.13–15 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979 by including an appropriate clause in the contract, regardless of whether the buyer is a consumer or a business. The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 only requires that the clause is written clearly — if it is, it binds the buyer automatically.'
answer: UCTA 1977 does NOT permit exclusion of ss.13–15 against consumers — CRA 2015 s.31 renders such terms not binding. Even in business-to-business sales, exclusion is only valid if the term satisfies the reasonableness test under s.11 UCTA 1977 — clarity alone is not enough.
feedback: Found two errors. First, a seller cannot exclude ss.13–15 against a consumer at all: CRA 2015 s.31 makes any such term not binding. Second, for business-to-business sales, UCTA 1977 s.6(2) subjects exclusion of ss.13–15 to the reasonableness test under s.11 and Schedule 2. A clearly drafted clause may still be unenforceable if it is unreasonable. Clarity is necessary but not sufficient.
oscoaReferences: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, ss.6(2), 11, Sch 2

question: Under UCTA 1977 and CRA 2015, which TWO of the following statements about excluding implied terms under the SGA 1979 are correct?

options.text: A business can exclude ss.13–15 SGA 1979 against a consumer if the exclusion clause is clearly drafted
options.feedback: Incorrect. CRA 2015 s.31 renders any term excluding the consumer's rights under ss.9–17 CRA (the consumer equivalents of ss.13–15 SGA) not binding on the consumer. Clarity is irrelevant — the exclusion is simply void.

options.text: Liability for breach of s.12 SGA 1979 (title) can never be excluded or restricted by any contract term, against any buyer
options.feedback: Correct. UCTA 1977 s.6(1) — s.12 is absolutely non-excludable. This applies whether the buyer is a consumer or a business. Title is fundamental: you cannot sell what you do not own and disclaim the consequences.

options.text: In a business-to-business sale, any exclusion of ss.13–15 is automatically enforceable if the buyer signed the contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. Signature may evidence that the buyer knew of the term (relevant to reasonableness), but it does not make an unreasonable term enforceable. The term must still satisfy the reasonableness test under UCTA 1977 s.11 and Schedule 2.

options.text: In a business-to-business contract, exclusion of ss.13–15 is only valid if the term satisfies the reasonableness test under UCTA 1977
options.feedback: Correct. UCTA 1977 s.6(2) permits exclusion of ss.13–15 in business sales, but only insofar as the term satisfies the requirement of reasonableness under s.11. Schedule 2 guidelines inform the court's assessment.

options.text: An exclusion clause is always reasonable if it limits liability to the purchase price of the goods
options.feedback: Incorrect. *George Mitchell* [1983] — an exclusion clause limiting liability to the purchase price of defective cabbage seed (£201) was held unreasonable where the buyer's crop loss was over £61,000. A price-cap may be reasonable in some contexts, but not as an invariable rule.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). The key points: s.12 (title) is absolutely non-excludable under UCTA 1977 s.6(1); in business-to-business sales, ss.13–15 exclusion is possible but must pass the reasonableness test; in consumer sales, CRA 2015 prohibits exclusion of consumer-quality terms entirely.
oscoaReferences: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1979, ss.6, 11, Sch 2
George Mitchell (Chesterhall) Ltd v Finney Lock Seeds Ltd [1983] 2 AC 803

question: TechSupply Ltd sells a server to a small accountancy firm under its standard terms, which include: 'All warranties, conditions, and implied terms, whether statutory or otherwise, are hereby excluded to the fullest extent permitted by law.' The server is defective and corrupts the firm's client data, causing £100,000 in losses. Applying UCTA 1977:

options.text: The exclusion clause is valid — it uses standard wording and the buyer signed the contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. The phrase 'to the fullest extent permitted by law' does not convert an unreasonable exclusion into an enforceable one. The court must still assess reasonableness under s.11.

options.text: The exclusion clause is void because it attempts to exclude s.12 (title)
options.feedback: Incorrect. While the clause is broadly worded, it does not target s.12 specifically. The key issue is its attempt to exclude ss.13–15, which requires reasonableness under s.6(2).

options.text: The clause is likely unreasonable — a blanket exclusion of all warranties in a standard-form contract between parties of unequal bargaining power, where the buyer has no realistic opportunity to negotiate, is unlikely to satisfy the Schedule 2 guidelines
options.feedback: Correct. A non-negotiated, blanket exclusion of all statutory implied terms in standard terms, imposed by a commercial seller on a small buyer, is highly vulnerable to challenge under UCTA 1977 s.6(2). The Schedule 2 factors — particularly bargaining strength and whether the term was individually negotiated — point strongly toward unreasonableness.

options.text: The clause is valid because it is a business-to-business contract and UCTA does not apply to business buyers
options.feedback: Incorrect. UCTA 1977 s.6(2) expressly applies to business-to-business contracts. The buyer's status as a business does not exclude UCTA — it merely changes the test from absolute prohibition (consumer) to reasonableness.

feedback: Blanket exclusion clauses in standard-form contracts are the classic target of UCTA 1977. The courts are especially sceptical of clauses that purport to exclude 'all implied terms' without negotiation. Under Schedule 2: the relative bargaining strength favours the seller; the clause was not individually negotiated; the loss (£100,000) is grossly disproportionate to what the buyer could have known or insured against. The clause is almost certainly unreasonable and unenforceable.
oscoaReferences: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, ss.6(2), 11, Sch 2

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] A business sells a 'budget' washing machine to a landlord for a rental property. The machine is described as 'basic but fully functional.' Within three months, the drum bearing fails, making the machine unusable. The seller argues, 'It was a budget model — you get what you pay for.' Applying s.14(2) SGA 1979:

options.text: The seller is not liable — the budget price excluded the implied term of satisfactory quality
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.14(2) applies to all business sales regardless of price. Price is a factor in the assessment but does not eliminate the implied term.

options.text: The seller is likely in breach — a washing machine that fails within three months is unlikely to be of satisfactory quality even at a budget price; durability under s.14(2B)(e) sets a floor that this machine falls below
options.feedback: Correct. A 'budget' washing machine must still be durable for a reasonable period. Three months falls below the objective standard a reasonable person would expect, even accounting for the low price. s.14(2B)(e) — durability — is engaged.

options.text: The seller is liable only if the landlord is a consumer
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.14(2) applies to all sales in the course of a business, whether the buyer is a consumer or a business (including a landlord buying for a rental property). UCTA may affect exclusion clauses, but the implied term itself applies to all business sales.

options.text: The seller is not liable because the description 'basic but fully functional' was accurate at the time of sale
options.feedback: Incorrect. Satisfactory quality includes durability — goods must remain functional for a reasonable period after sale. A description of the goods at the point of sale does not override the ongoing durability requirement.

feedback: This consolidates Block A. The s.14(2) implied term applies to ALL business sales. Price is relevant — under s.14(2A), a reasonable person's expectation of a budget item is lower — but a washing machine that fails within three months falls below any reasonable floor. Durability (s.14(2B)(e)) is the key factor here. A budget item may have fewer features, but it must still perform its basic function for a reasonable period.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] The case of Rogers v __________ (Scarborough) Ltd [1987] QB 933 is authority that a new vehicle with multiple defects — including oil leaks, defective paintwork, and engine problems — is not of satisfactory quality under s.14(2) SGA 1979, even if the defects can be repaired.
feedback: Correct. *Rogers v Parish (Scarborough) Ltd* [1987] QB 933 — the accumulation of defects rendered the Range Rover unsatisfactory. The obligation is that goods are of satisfactory quality at delivery; the possibility of repair does not cure the breach. This reinforces the s.14(2) standard.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] Which TWO of the following correctly state the legal requirements for the implied term of fitness for a particular purpose under s.14(3) SGA 1979?

options.text: The buyer must make known to the seller, expressly or by implication, the particular purpose for which the goods are required
options.feedback: Correct. s.14(3) — this is the foundational requirement. The buyer's purpose must be communicated. It can be an ordinary purpose (*Grant v AKM* [1936] — wearing underwear) or a specialist one (*Baldry v Marshall* [1925] — a touring car).

options.text: The buyer must prove the goods are also unsatisfactory under s.14(2) before s.14(3) is engaged
options.feedback: Incorrect. The two subsections are independent. A buyer may succeed under s.14(3) alone if the goods are unfit for the particular purpose, even if they would otherwise be of 'satisfactory quality' under s.14(2).

options.text: The particular purpose must be unusual or beyond the ordinary use of such goods
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Grant v Australian Knitting Mills* [1936] AC 85 — the purpose of wearing underwear on skin is an entirely ordinary purpose, yet it satisfies the 'particular purpose' requirement under s.14(3). The purpose need only be the specific purpose the buyer communicates.

options.text: The buyer must rely, or it must be reasonable for the buyer to rely, on the seller's skill or judgment
options.feedback: Correct. s.14(3) — the reliance requirement. Partial reliance is sufficient. The buyer may also conduct their own research, provided the seller's skill/judgment remained a factor in the decision.

options.text: The seller's liability is limited to goods sold to consumers — s.14(3) does not apply to business buyers
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.14(3) applies to ALL sales in the course of a business, whoever the buyer is. Business buyers can and do rely on s.14(3). UCTA 1977 may affect the ability to exclude it, but the implied term itself applies universally.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (d). These are the two core elements of s.14(3): the buyer communicates the purpose, and the buyer relies (or it is reasonable to rely) on the seller's skill or judgment. The purpose may be ordinary; s.14(3) stands independently of s.14(2); partial reliance suffices.
oscoaReferences: Grant v Australian Knitting Mills [1936] AC 85

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block C] A software company's standard licence terms state: 'All implied terms, including those under the Sale of Goods Act 1979, are excluded. The company's sole liability is to use reasonable efforts to correct defects.' A small charity buys the software and it is riddled with bugs, making it unusable. The charity had no opportunity to negotiate the terms. Applying UCTA 1977, s.6(2):

options.text: The exclusion is likely unreasonable — standard-form terms imposed on a party with no bargaining power, with no opportunity to negotiate, strongly indicate unreasonableness under Schedule 2
options.feedback: Correct. Under UCTA 1977 Schedule 2, the court considers bargaining strength and whether the term was individually negotiated. A charity with no bargaining power, presented with non-negotiable standard terms, is precisely the scenario where exclusion clauses are struck down as unreasonable.

options.text: The exclusion is automatically void because the buyer is a charity
options.feedback: Incorrect. UCTA 1977 does not create special status for charities. However, the charity's lack of bargaining power is a factor pointing toward unreasonableness. The test is the same for all non-consumer buyers.

options.text: The exclusion is valid — the company's 'reasonable efforts' obligation provides adequate protection
options.feedback: Incorrect. A promise to use 'reasonable efforts to correct defects' is not an adequate substitute for the implied terms under ss.13–15 SGA. Under *George Mitchell* [1983], a token alternative remedy does not save an otherwise unreasonable exclusion.

options.text: The exclusion is valid because business-to-business contracts are exempt from UCTA 1977
options.feedback: Incorrect. UCTA 1977 s.6(2) explicitly applies to business-to-business contracts. The only difference is that exclusion is tested for reasonableness rather than being absolutely prohibited (as it is against consumers under CRA 2015).

feedback: This consolidates Block C. The UCTA reasonableness test is context-sensitive. Key factors from Schedule 2 mapped to this scenario: (a) significant inequality of bargaining strength, (b) no inducement to accept the term, (c) the charity could not practically have known about it in advance (standard terms, no negotiation), and (d) the goods were not manufactured to special order. Taken together, these factors strongly indicate the exclusion is unreasonable and unenforceable.
oscoaReferences: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, ss.6(2), 11, Sch 2
George Mitchell (Chesterhall) Ltd v Finney Lock Seeds Ltd [1983] 2 AC 803

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block C] In a business-to-business sale, liability under ss.13–15 SGA 1979 may be excluded only if the term satisfies the __________ test under s.11 of the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977.
feedback: Correct. UCTA 1977, s.6(2) read with s.11 — a term excluding liability under ss.13–15 SGA in a business-to-business sale is enforceable only insofar as it satisfies the requirement of reasonableness. The test is whether the term was 'a fair and reasonable one to be included' having regard to the circumstances known to the parties at the time of contracting. Schedule 2 provides non-exhaustive guidelines.
oscoaReferences: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, ss.6(2), 11, Sch 2
